summary: Problem 3 asks whether a nontrivial Markov chain exists on S_n(lambda) whose stationary distribution is given by ratios of interpolation ASEP polynomials and interpolation Macdonald polynomials at q=1, for restricted partitions with distinct parts. The current best attempt produced no solution.tex; the interactive solve was stopped without progress, and the low solvability score (13%) reflects that this requires constructing a genuinely new object rather than verifying an existing result.
problems: No solution.tex was produced — the solver stopped during interactive solving with no formal proof or construction in hand.

The core mathematical blocker is the absence of an explicit candidate Markov chain: the problem demands construction of new transition rules whose stationary measure matches a specific ratio of interpolation Macdonald/ASEP polynomials.

The stationary distribution target involves interpolation Macdonald polynomials specialized at q=1, which are technically demanding objects with limited ready-to-use combinatorial transition-rule machinery.
Status reporting is inconsistent ('100 open issues' / '100 open issues' vs. a single resolved issue), making it hard to assess true remaining workload.
The restricted-partition constraints (unique part of size 0, no part of size 1) have not been exploited or even analyzed in any recorded attempt.
highlights: The problem is well-scoped with precise combinatorial constraints (distinct parts, restricted partition) that can structure a candidate construction.
There is a clear connection to known ASEP and interpolation Macial polynomial frameworks (Cantini–de Gier–Wheeler, Corteel–Mandelshtam–Williams) that provide a starting toolkit.
The single recorded issue is marked resolved, indicating administrative cleanup is largely done even if mathematical content is missing.

suggested_todos.title: Define an explicit candidate Markov chain on S_n(lambda) with proposed transition rates and write it into a new solution.tex skeleton
Compute the q=1 specialization of the relevant interpolation Macdonald/ASEP polynomials for small n (n=2,3) to obtain a concrete target stationary distribution
Verify the candidate chain's stationary distribution against the small-n targets numerically before attempting a general proof
Analyze how the restricted-partition conditions (no part of size 1, unique 0) constrain or simplify the state space and transitions
Survey ASEP/interpolation-Macdonald literature (Corteel–Mandelshtam–Williams, Cantini–de Gier–Wheeler) for transposition-style Markov chains adaptable to this setting
Reconcile the conflicting issue counts (100 open vs. 1 resolved) in the tracker for accurate status
